summary: Martin Fowler argues that enterprises should move beyond the assumption that relational databases are the default choice for data persistence. Drawing on Neal Ford's concept of polyglot programming, he proposes that organizations should use different storage technologies for different types of data based on how that data will be manipulated. While acknowledging the complexity costs of managing multiple persistence technologies, he contends the benefits—reduced development drag and better scalability—make this shift worthwhile for strategic projects. Fowler predicts this 'database thaw' will progress steadily over the coming decade, though companies will adopt it conservatively.
key_points: For new strategic enterprise applications, relational persistence should no longer be the default assumption—alternatives must be seriously evaluated
Polyglot persistence will appear even within single applications, with different components using different storage technologies based on data manipulation needs
The costs include learning new interfaces, understanding performance characteristics, and grappling with consistency/availability tradeoffs on clusters
Strategic projects suffering from development drag or intensive data needs are the best candidates for alternative persistence
This shift will be gradual due to natural conservatism around data storage, but the trend is inevitable
money_quotes: I'm confident to say that if you starting a new strategic enterprise application you should no longer be assuming that your persistence should be relational.
Increasingly we'll be first asking how we want to manipulate the data and only then figuring out what technology is the best bet for it.
The transactional single point of truth will no longer hold sway (although its role as such has often been illusory).
When relational databases are used inappropriately, they exert a significant drag on application development.
Polyglot persistence will come at a cost - but it will come because the benefits are worth it.
A component boundary is a good way to wrap a particular storage technology chosen for the way its data in manipulated.
key_insight: The question is no longer whether to use a relational database, but how you want to manipulate your data—and only then choosing the storage technology that best fits.
